extra.reasoning: The property requires that rec_drug_a and rec_drug_b are never jointly true. Two rules are both triggered by the single input 'anticoagulated': r3 (priority 50) sets rec_drug_a := true, and r1 (priority 50) sets rec_drug_b := true. There are no competing rules for either output, so priorities/tie-breaking are irrelevant — both rules fire unopposed whenever anticoagulated is true, and the unset-default only applies when no rule sets an output. Therefore any input with anticoagulated = true makes both rec_drug_a = true and rec_drug_b = true. The other inputs (g6pd_deficiency, immunosuppressed) only affect rec_drug_c and are irrelevant to this property. Counterexample: anticoagulated = true, g6pd_deficiency = false, immunosuppressed = false yields rec_drug_a = true, rec_drug_b = true, violating mutual exclusion. The plain-language intent (do not recommend both drug a and drug b) is also clearly violated for an anticoagulated patient.
